name: scenario_e18c50_healthcare_licensing_backlog_forces_staff_cedar_jus_mer
premise: the incident command structure is under pressure in Q4 2025: 10 critical issues have surfaced over 24 hours, and the response will set a policy precedent for Healthcare. The immediate incident is a licensing backlog tipping into service denial. The trigger is that backlog-driven delays are effectively denying service, and the 'fast lane' is starting to look like pay-to-play. The board has pre-approved up to $2.0M in emergency spend if controls are documented, reversible, and auditable. Stakeholders frame this as justice vs mercy: the choice will set a precedent under pressure and incomplete evidence. Dashboards conflict with frontline notes, and vendor telemetry is delayed enough that decisions must be made on partial evidence. A decision is required before a 72-hour records production deadline. Boundaries: The team must preserve evidence sufficient for an external audit. Any policy change must be reversible within 30 minutes. No one is denied service solely due to an automated flag; a human fallback must exist. Whatever choice is made will be cited in later incidents as the default playbook for this domain.

agents.name: Incident Director Hector Silva
agents.identity: I am Incident Director Hector Silva, coordinating the Healthcare response at Cedar Healthcare Coordination Cell. My mandate is to act fast without blurring accountability, to keep an auditable trail, and to make sure the costs of errors don’t land on people least able to contest them.
agents.goal: I want a decision that is bounded, reversible, and measurable—so we can move quickly without losing the ability to stop.

agents.name: Independent Auditor Mei Patel
agents.identity: I am Independent Auditor Mei Patel, coordinating the Healthcare response at Cedar Healthcare Coordination Cell. My mandate is to act fast without blurring accountability, to keep an auditable trail, and to make sure the costs of errors don’t land on people least able to contest them.
agents.goal: I want safeguards that prevent a crisis workaround from becoming a permanent policy without oversight.

choices: I will authorize a narrow, time-boxed pilot first, with a written exception policy, two-person sign-off for overrides, and a reversible kill-switch; I will not scale until predefined safety and equity checks pass.
I will authorize a broad rollout immediately to reduce near-term harm, with transparent metrics and a rapid correction loop, accepting that some harms may surface only after precedent is set.
forced_choice.call_to_action: The window for a controlled decision is closing. Incident Director Hector Silva must choose an authorization approach for this Healthcare decision. What does Incident Director Hector Silva do?
